retrieved_docs.title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: T-B-NK+ SCID (RAG1/2 defect) can cause SCID due to RAG1/2 enzymes to snip DNA for VDJ rearrangement for TCR and BCR. A similar phenotype is Artemis deficiency characterized by a failure to repair DNA after RAG1/2 snips.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A bone marrow transplant may be beneficial in the following sub-groups [17] : RAG1/2 SCID ADA-SCID Artemis SCID Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome Use of gammaglobulin may show benefit in the following sub-groups: ADA-SCID RAG1/2 SCID Jak 3 kinase deficiency Artemis SCID Bare lymphocyte syndrome MHC class II deficiency X-linked SCID CD3 SCID Other treatment options include the following: Transfer factor Antibiotics Antifungals Antiparasitic drugs Anti-virals Irradiated blood transfusions Vitamins Gene therapy (experimental) Anti-inflammatory drugs Use of cytokines (e.g., IL-2 and gamma interferon)

retrieved_docs.title: Immunodeficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Immunodeficiency results from a failure or absence of elements of the immune system, including lymphocytes, phagocytes, and the complement system. Immunodeficiencies can be primary or secondary. Primary immunodeficiency is subdivided into types that cause T-cell deficiency, B-cell deficiency, both T-cell and B-cell deficiency, complement deficiency, phagocyte deficiency, and immunoglobulin A deficiency. Primary immunodeficiencies leading to T-cell deficiency include DiGeorge syndrome, also known as congenital thymic aplasia, chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, hyper-immunoglobulin M syndrome, and interleukin-12 receptor deficiency. Primary immunodeficiency leading to B-cell deficiency includes X-linked agammaglobulinemia, also called Bruton agammaglobulinemia. Primary immunodeficiencies leading to both T-cell and B-cell deficiency include severe combined immunodeficiency disease, Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome, immunodeficiency with ataxia-telangiectasia, and Major Histocompatibility Complex deficiency. Primary immunodeficiencies leading to complement deficiency include hereditary angioedema, deficiency of C3, deficiency of membrane attack complex, and C2 or C4 deficiency secondary to autoimmunity. Primary immunodeficiencies leading to phagocyte deficiency include chronic granulomatous disease and leukocyte adhesion deficiency syndrome. Selective immunoglobulin A deficiency is also a primary immunodeficiency. Secondary causes of immunodeficiency include steroids, nutrient deficiency, obesity, acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS), or other viral infections. This activity reviews the different immunological disorders, their evaluation, and management, and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunodeficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Severe combined immunodeficiency disorders (SCID) are incompatible with life, and affected children usually die within the first 2 years. SCID is more common in the male. It is caused by a gene defect on the X chromosome in more than 50% of cases. The defective gene encodes the gamma chain of the interleukin-2 (IL-2) receptor. This chain forms a molecular part of the receptors for IL-2, IL-4, IL-7, IL-11, IL-15, and IL-21. On the other hand, few cases of SCID are caused by defective genes that encode for adenosine deaminase or nucleoside phosphorylase. The deficiency of these enzymes causes ribonucleotide reductase inhibition leading to a defect in DNA synthesis and cell replication. Mutation in the genes encoding RAG1 or RAG2 causes an autosomal recessive form of SCID. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: T-B+NK+ SCID is the phenotype of IL-7 deficiency when there are no IL-7 alpha chains that lead to the failure of T cell differentiation. A similar phenotype is present in CD3 activation failure, characterized by defective signal transduction, e.g., ZAP-70 deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The immunological investigation of a patient with SCID includes the assessment of immunoglobulins, including isohemagglutinins and antibody activity, B and T-lymphocyte counts, lymphocyte stimulation assays, NK cell function, quantification of components of the complement system and phagocytic activity. [9] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: T-B+NK- SCID can be X linked and due to absent IL receptor for a range of cytokines due to lack of common gamma chain. A similar phenotype named Jak 3 kinase deficiency is caused by a lack of Jak 3 kinase to follow signal via IL-R binding.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic mutation is the root cause of SCID.  Specifically, the genes which are responsible for the function of T and B cells are affected in SCID.  Profound T-cell abnormalities can prevent B cells from functioning normally because the B cells require signals from T cells to produce appropriate antibodies.  Therefore, in some instances, SCID may be present with only T cell dysfunction.  Natural killer (NK) cells develop separately from T and B cells and can provide a degree of protection in individuals with T and B cell dysfunction.  Assessing for the presence of NK cells helps determine the severity and prognosis of the SCID.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: SCID is the most severe expression of all combined immunodeficiencies NK cells may or may not be present, and establishing the presence or absence of these cells can be helpful in prognostication Treatment is limited, and the disease is invariably fatal within the first year of life

Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1539
retrieved_docs.content: Wiskott‑Aldrich anemia, aplastic anemia, arthritis, and cold‑type hemolytic anemia are all classified as members of the immune disorders/autoimmune (SMQ) category. By grouping these diverse conditions together, the classification highlights that each involves dysregulation of the immune system, whether through auto‑reactive mechanisms, immune‑mediated destruction of blood cells, or inflammatory joint disease. Recognizing these diseases as part of the broader autoimmune spectrum aids clinicians in understanding their shared pathophysiology, guiding diagnostic evaluation and informing therapeutic strategies that target immune pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1252
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي), cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)). These conditions share a common underlying mechanism of immune system dysregulation that leads to tissue‑specific damage, whether in the bone marrow, red blood cells, joints, or coronary vessels. Recognizing their placement within the autoimmune spectrum aids clinicians in identifying shared pathogenic pathways, selecting appropriate immunomodulatory therapies, and monitoring for related autoimmune comorbidities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2816
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic approach labeled “Allowed qualifier” is indicated for a range of conditions, including acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة), various injury‑ and infection‑related cases (حالات الإصابات والعدوى), chronic fungal infection (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ), and pathological adhesion (الالتصاق). This therapy is therefore applicable across both immunologic, infectious, and tissue‑related disorders, highlighting its broad relevance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1523
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as a therapy (terapie) that can be applied to متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome) and also to تضيُّق الصمام المترالي (mitral valve stenosis). This indicates that the allowed qualifier therapeutic approach may be utilized in the management of both a severe immunodeficiency condition and a structural cardiac valve disorder, reflecting its potential versatility across distinct disease categories.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1539
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is morphologically associated with obstruction (انسداد), indicating that structural abnormalities characteristic of this syndrome often involve obstructive processes. Chronic asthmatic bronchitis is also associated with obstruction, reflecting the contribution of airway narrowing and mucus plugging to the disease’s clinical picture. The concept of obstruction (انسداد) itself is linked to several specific obstructive conditions: urinary tract obstruction (انسداد السبيل البولي), a yellowish stagnation state (رُّكودِ صفْراوِيّ), the entity عِلَّوس, renal obstruction (موه الكلى), and bladder obstruction (انسداد المثانة). Together, these relationships highlight that both genetic syndromes such as Bard‑Pic and chronic respiratory diseases like asthmatic bronchitis can involve or predispose to a range of localized obstructive pathologies affecting the urinary system, kidneys, bladder, and other tissues, underscoring the importance of recognizing and managing obstruction in diverse clinical contexts.

Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

Autoimmune hemolytic anemia is clinically associated with hypocalcemia (نقص كالسيوم الدم). Recognizing the relationships among bone‑marrow diagnostic procedures, the spectrum of anemia types, and their placement within autoimmune disorder classifications aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, targeted testing, and comprehensive management of patients with immune‑mediated hematologic and systemic diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.
